title: Data Engineer Technical Round
description: Five scenario questions. Each question focuses on one practical data engineering decision.

questions.title: Simple Sales Data Model

scenario.context: A retailer wants a daily report of sales amount and quantity by product and region. Product categories can change over time.
constraints: Historical reports should keep the category that applied when the sale occurred.
The model should be simple for analysts to query.
scenario.prompt: State the grain of the sales data, the main fact and dimension tables, and how you would preserve product-category history.

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate understands grain, basic dimensional modeling, and historical attributes.

target_elements.description: States what one fact row represents.
Identifies a sales fact and the main dimensions and measures.
Explains a simple way to preserve past product categories.

probes.trigger: The candidate names tables but does not define a grain.
probes.prompt: What exactly does one row in the sales fact represent?

probes.trigger: The candidate does not address category changes.
probes.prompt: How would last month's sales keep last month's product category?

hint_ladder: Start by defining one row in the fact table.
Then name the measures, dimensions, and one method for keeping old category values.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Focuses on the requested report and historical requirement.

anchors.5: Clearly connects the model to both reporting and history.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Defines a sensible grain, fact, dimensions, and history approach.

anchors.5: Provides a precise, compact model with a sound history method.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Keeps measures and historical categories correct.

anchors.1: Would produce ambiguous or changing historical totals.
anchors.3: Keeps the main totals and history correct.
anchors.5: Also mentions a simple key or reconciliation check.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Briefly justifies the chosen grain or history method.

anchors.1: Gives no reason for the design.
anchors.5: Explains the main benefit and cost concisely.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: Explains the model clearly and briefly.

anchors.1: The answer is difficult to follow.
anchors.5: The answer is structured, precise, and concise.

strong_answer_outline: Use one row per order line or sale line so quantity and sales amount have a clear grain.
Create a sales fact with date, product, and region keys plus quantity and amount; use separate date, product, and region dimensions.
Keep category history with versioned product rows, such as a Type 2 slowly changing dimension, so each sale points to the category valid at that time.
acceptable_alternatives: A daily product-region aggregate is acceptable if the candidate states that transaction-level drill-down is not needed.
A dated category-history table is acceptable if the as-of join is explained correctly.
red_flags: Measures with different grains are mixed together.
The current category overwrites required history.

questions.title: Daily Multi-Source Pipeline

scenario.context: A daily dashboard combines customer data from an operational database with a partner CSV file. The CSV sometimes arrives late, and jobs may be retried.
constraints: The dashboard only needs a daily refresh.
A retry must not duplicate data.
scenario.prompt: Outline a simple batch pipeline from the two sources to the dashboard, and explain how you would handle a late file and a job retry.

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate can describe a basic reliable batch pipeline without over-designing it.

target_elements.description: Lands both inputs with basic source and run metadata.
Validates, combines, and publishes a complete daily output.
Handles retries without duplicates and defines behavior for a late file.

probes.trigger: The candidate says to rerun the job but does not address duplicates.
probes.prompt: What makes the rerun safe if part of the output was already written?

probes.trigger: The candidate does not explain late-file behavior.
probes.prompt: Would you wait, publish partial data, or keep the last good version, and why?

hint_ladder: Think in three steps: land, transform, publish.
Include one rule for safe retries and one rule for late files.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Recognizes the daily SLA and the two main failure cases.

anchors.1: Ignores the SLA or failure cases.
anchors.5: Clearly frames the daily cutoff, late file, and retry behavior.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Provides a coherent, simple source-to-dashboard flow.

anchors.1: Lists tools without a data flow.
anchors.3: Explains a workable landing, transform, and publish flow.
anchors.5: Adds clear run state and publication behavior without unnecessary complexity.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Prevents duplicate or incomplete daily output.

anchors.1: Retries can duplicate data or expose partial output.
anchors.3: Uses an idempotent retry and sensible late-file rule.
anchors.5: Also includes a simple validation and alert.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Explains the freshness-versus-completeness choice.

anchors.5: Ties the choice directly to dashboard expectations.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: Explains the pipeline clearly and briefly.

anchors.1: The answer is difficult to follow.
anchors.5: The normal flow and both failure cases are concise and clear.

strong_answer_outline: Extract the database changes and land the CSV unchanged with date, source, and run metadata.
Validate both inputs, standardize and join them, then publish the daily dataset only after required checks pass.
Use a stable business key and merge or replace the affected date so retries are idempotent; if the file is late, alert and either wait or keep the last good output according to the agreed cutoff.
acceptable_alternatives: A managed connector or a custom extractor is acceptable; the answer only needs a clear, safe batch flow.
Publishing a clearly marked partial dataset is acceptable if the business explicitly prefers freshness over completeness.
red_flags: No retained source copy or run metadata.
A retry blindly appends the same data.
Partial output is published without warning.

questions.title: Revenue Drop Check

scenario.context: A dashboard shows revenue down 30 percent, but the refresh job says it succeeded and the business knows of no reason for the drop.
constraints: Do not change or rerun data blindly.
scenario.prompt: What are the first three checks you would make, and how would you safely restore the dashboard if the data is wrong?

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate can debug a data issue in a logical order and validate a repair.

target_elements.description: Verifies the metric, filters, dates, and source-level total.
Compares data across pipeline layers to find the first mismatch.
Contains, repairs, validates, and communicates the issue.

probes.trigger: The candidate assumes the source is missing data.
probes.prompt: The source total is correct. What do you compare next?

probes.trigger: The candidate proposes a rerun without a validation step.
probes.prompt: How would you prove the repaired dashboard is correct?

hint_ladder: Check the dashboard definition, then compare totals backward through the pipeline.
Find the first mismatched layer before repairing only the affected data.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Confirms the reported symptom and its scope.

anchors.5: Also checks filters, dates, and affected slices.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Uses layer-by-layer comparisons to find the fault.

anchors.5: Efficiently identifies the first layer where totals diverge.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Repairs and validates data safely.

anchors.3: Repairs the affected data and checks totals.
anchors.5: Also contains bad output and adds a targeted check.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Balances fast restoration with confidence in the result.

anchors.5: Uses a simple containment step while investigating.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: Explains actions and stakeholder updates clearly.

anchors.5: Also states confidence and the next update.

strong_answer_outline: Check the dashboard metric, filters, date range, and cache, then compare the displayed total with the serving table and source total.
Compare row counts and revenue totals from serving data back through transformed and raw data to find the first mismatched layer; check recent code, schema, and job logs there.
Hide or flag the bad output, repair or rerun only the affected period, reconcile the result to the source, notify stakeholders, and add a check for the discovered failure.
acceptable_alternatives: Keeping the last known good dashboard is acceptable if it is clearly marked stale.
A genuine business decline is a valid conclusion when every layer reconciles.
red_flags: Reruns everything without first locating the problem.
States a root cause without evidence.
Declares recovery without reconciling the result.

questions.title: Pipeline Slowdown

scenario.context: A daily pipeline used to take 20 minutes and now takes 2 hours after data volume increased. The output is still correct, and an execution profile is available.
constraints: Runtime and compute cost both matter.
The fix must preserve output correctness.
scenario.prompt: What would you inspect first, and give one or two likely fixes based on what the execution profile shows?

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate can connect basic performance evidence to a targeted fix.

target_elements.description: Compares a fast and slow run, including volume and recent changes.
Uses the execution profile to identify the slow stage and likely cause.
Chooses a matching fix and checks runtime, cost, and correctness.

probes.trigger: The candidate does not examine task-level behavior.
probes.prompt: If one task takes much longer than all others, what might that indicate?

probes.trigger: The candidate proposes a fix but no measurement.
probes.prompt: What would you compare before and after the change?

hint_ladder: Find which stage accounts for the extra time.
Match the fix to evidence such as extra scanning, skew, shuffle, or a full refresh.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Establishes a useful before-and-after comparison.

anchors.5: Also checks recent code or configuration changes.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Reads the profile and proposes a cause-specific fix.

anchors.3: Finds a plausible slow stage and fix.
anchors.5: Clearly connects profile evidence, root cause, and fix.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Checks that optimized output remains correct.

anchors.3: Compares key totals before and after.
anchors.5: Also keeps a rollback or regression check.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Considers runtime, cost, and complexity.

anchors.5: Chooses the simplest effective fix and states its tradeoff.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: Explains the diagnosis and fix concisely.

anchors.3: Gives a clear diagnosis and fix.
anchors.5: Connects evidence, action, and expected result directly.

strong_answer_outline: Compare a recent fast run with the slow run: input size, code or configuration changes, and which stage now dominates runtime.
Use the profile to look for excess scanning, a large shuffle, skewed tasks, join growth, spills, or full recomputation.
Apply a matching fix, such as earlier filtering, better partition pruning, handling a skewed key, or incremental processing, then compare runtime, cost, and output totals.
acceptable_alternatives: Adding compute is acceptable when the profile shows a real capacity limit and the cost is justified.
Compaction or a join change is acceptable when file or join evidence supports it.
red_flags: Adds resources before checking the profile.
Suggests many unrelated optimizations at once.
Does not verify cost or data correctness.

questions.title: Unexpected Schema Change

scenario.context: A partner API adds a nullable field and changes customer_id from an integer to a string. Two downstream models still expect an integer.
constraints: Incoming source records must not be lost.
Some new customer IDs may contain letters.
scenario.prompt: Which change is safe, which is breaking, and what short migration plan would you use to keep the pipeline running without losing data?

questions.objective: Assess whether the candidate can classify schema changes and describe a safe, simple migration.

target_elements.description: Distinguishes the additive nullable field from the breaking identifier change.
Preserves raw inputs and avoids silent loss or unsafe coercion.
Moves downstream consumers to a compatible string identifier and reprocesses affected records.

probes.trigger: The candidate suggests casting every new ID back to an integer.
probes.prompt: What happens when a new customer ID contains letters?

probes.trigger: The candidate only discusses future records.
probes.prompt: What would you do with changed records that already landed?

hint_ladder: Preserve raw data, introduce a compatible identifier, update consumers, and replay affected records.

dimensions.name: problem_framing
dimensions.description: Identifies the affected values and consumers.

anchors.5: Also checks actual values and affected consumers.

dimensions.name: technical_depth
dimensions.description: Provides a workable compatibility and migration approach.

anchors.3: Uses a string identifier and updates consumers.
anchors.5: Also explains a simple transition path.

dimensions.name: data_correctness_reliability
dimensions.description: Preserves inputs and safely handles affected records.

anchors.3: Preserves raw data and reprocesses affected records.
anchors.5: Also validates counts, keys, and downstream joins.

dimensions.name: tradeoff_reasoning
dimensions.description: Balances continuity with downstream compatibility.

anchors.1: Either blocks everything or accepts everything silently.
anchors.5: Uses a short compatibility window with a clear end state.

dimensions.name: communication
dimensions.description: Explains the migration clearly and briefly.

anchors.5: The classification, containment, and migration are concise and ordered.

strong_answer_outline: Treat the new nullable field as generally backward compatible, but treat customer_id changing to string as breaking because new values may not convert to integers.
Keep the original payload, reject or quarantine unsafe transformed records rather than dropping them, and alert the data owner.
Adopt string as the canonical customer ID, provide a temporary compatibility field or view while the two models are updated, then replay and validate the affected records.
acceptable_alternatives: A versioned output model or dual columns are both acceptable transition methods.
Pausing trusted publication is acceptable if raw ingestion continues and consumers are notified.
red_flags: Casts every ID to integer and loses alphanumeric values.
Updates the schema without migrating or notifying consumers.
